competitions.title: Cambridge Re:Think Essay Competition 2026
competitions.shortDesc: An international essay competition for secondary-school students encouraging critical thinking and exploration of diverse, thought-provoking topics.
competitions.fullDetails: The competition is offered in two age-based categories: Junior Division (ages 11–13) and Senior Division (ages 14–18), focusing on intellectual rigor, originality, and clarity. It invites students worldwide to engage deeply with historical, present, and speculative future scenarios, fostering critical analysis and diverse perspectives. Entry is free. The Award Ceremony and Dinner will be held at the University of Cambridge.
competitions.location: Online submission; Award Ceremony at University of Cambridge, UK
competitions.prizes: Awards for top entries (representing top 0.25% globally), Honourable Mentions (top 10%), and an invitation to the Award Ceremony in Cambridge.
competitions.rounds: One submission round, followed by judging.
competitions.link: Not directly provided in snippet, but mentioned 'Learn More about the 2026 Competition' on the Cambridge Re:Think Essay Competition website.

competitions.title: 2026 NCSS Student Leadership & Research Conference
competitions.shortDesc: A conference building on student research while expanding its focus to include STEM leadership development and entrepreneurship for students.
competitions.fullDetails: This conference connects rigorous research to collaboration, communication, innovation, and real-world impact within STEM. Students will present original research, participate in STEM leadership sessions, entrepreneurship-focused programming, and engage in conversations with active researchers, including visits to leading university research facilities.
competitions.location: Fulton Science Academy, Tech North Atlanta, Georgia Tech Research Institute
competitions.prizes: Opportunities for leadership development, networking, and presenting original research.
competitions.fee: NCSS Member Schools: $50 per student; Non-NCSS Member Schools: $100 per student.

competitions.title: Global Impact Competition 2026
competitions.shortDesc: An international competition challenging high school students to investigate community issues through field research and policy writing.
competitions.fullDetails: This competition invites students worldwide to kickstart meaningful passion projects and collaborate with peers. It encourages an in-depth exploration of a chosen community issue, culminating in policy writing.
competitions.prizes: Up to $250 cash prize and potential internship opportunities with a global strategic innovation consultancy.

competitions.title: Global Quantum Mechanics Challenge (GQMC)
competitions.shortDesc: An international, research-oriented challenge designed for high school and university students interested in the quantum world.
competitions.fullDetails: The Global Quantum Mechanics Challenge offers an accessible platform for students to delve into quantum mechanics. It culminates in a final round where top achievers are awarded cash prizes.
competitions.prizes: Cash prizes: 1st Prize Senior: $250 USD, 2nd Prize Senior: $175 USD.

competitions.title: 2026 International Youth Competition
competitions.shortDesc: An international competition inviting students to share ideas and perspectives on social challenges through visual art, essays, or open submissions.
competitions.fullDetails: The 2026 International Youth Competition, with the theme 'Why Me?', is open to students from primary, middle, and high school, as well as college. Participants can enter individually or as teams, submitting visual art (drawings, illustrations, paintings, photos), essays (persuasive or creative, with English translation if non-English), or open ideas. AI-generated works are not accepted.
competitions.eventDate: Winners announced by end of September 2026.
competitions.prizes: Awards for 1st, 2nd, and 3rd place in each category and division, plus 'Distinguished Achievement' certificates for all participants.

competitions.title: Regeneron International Science and Engineering Fair (ISEF) 2026
competitions.shortDesc: The world's largest and most prestigious pre-collegiate science research competition for high school students.
competitions.fullDetails: High school students (grades 9-12) conduct original research projects and first compete at regional and state-level fairs to qualify for the international competition. ISEF helps students develop advanced research, critical thinking, and presentation skills. Top projects receive global recognition, significant scholarships, and advantages for college and STEM career pathways.
competitions.deadline: First round typically Jan/Feb 2026 (for regional fairs).
competitions.prizes: Prizes in the tens of thousands, including the $50,000 Regeneron Young Scientist Award.
competitions.rounds: Regional/state fairs to qualify for the international final.
competitions.fee: Not explicitly stated for direct entry; regional fairs may have fees.
competitions.link: ScienceFair.io provides resources and coaching for ISEF.

competitions.title: Conrad Challenge 2026-2027
competitions.shortDesc: An international competition for high school students to develop innovative solutions to real-world problems in STEM and entrepreneurship.
competitions.fullDetails: Teams of up to five students (ages 13-18) develop solutions across categories like aerospace, energy, and health. The challenge emphasizes entrepreneurship alongside STEM, involving brainstorming, expert feedback, and a potential Innovation Summit.
competitions.eventDate: The 2025-2026 Innovation Summit is in April 2026. Dates for 2026-2027 summit TBD.
competitions.location: Innovation Summit for finalists at the Houston Space Center.
competitions.prizes: Not explicitly detailed, but recognition and opportunities for innovation.

competitions.title: Breakthrough Junior Challenge 2026
competitions.shortDesc: An international competition inviting students to create a two-minute video explaining a complex scientific concept or theory.
competitions.fullDetails: Open to students aged 13-18 worldwide, participants must submit an original two-minute video in English, creatively explaining a scientific concept or theory. The winner receives significant recognition and the opportunity to attend the prestigious Breakthrough Prize ceremony.
competitions.location: Online submission; winner attends ceremony in California.
competitions.prizes: Opportunity to attend the Breakthrough Prize ceremony in California and meet influential leaders in science and technology.

competitions.title: Congressional App Challenge 2026
competitions.shortDesc: A nationwide STEM competition for middle and high school students in the U.S. to design and develop their own original apps.
competitions.fullDetails: Sponsored by members of the U.S. Congress, this challenge encourages students to use technology to improve their community. Students can use any programming language (e.g., C, C++, Javascript, Python, Ruby) and any platform (e.g., PC, tablet, mobile). Winners are selected at the congressional district level and invited to present on Capitol Hill.
competitions.eventDate: Winners present on Capitol Hill (date TBD).
competitions.location: US-based (online submission); winners invited to Washington D.C.
competitions.prizes: Recognition and opportunity to present to congressional members.

competitions.title: FIRST Robotics Competition (FRC) 2026
competitions.shortDesc: High school student teams design, build, and program industrial-size robots to compete in a new game challenge each year.
competitions.fullDetails: The 2026 season theme is 'REBUILT'. Teams have approximately six weeks to build their robots, then compete in regional and district events, culminating in the FIRST Championship. The competition teaches engineering, programming, fundraising, project management, marketing, and teamwork.
competitions.deadline: Season kicked off 2026-01-10; regional/district events run through April 2026.
competitions.eventDate: Regional/District events: Jan-Apr 2026; FIRST Championship: TBD (typically April).
competitions.location: Various regional/district events; FIRST Championship in Houston, USA.
competitions.prizes: Not explicitly detailed, but recognition and skill development.
competitions.fee: Not explicitly stated, but teams generally incur costs.

competitions.title: Genius Olympiad 2026
competitions.shortDesc: An international high school competition hosted in New York, focusing on projects that connect to the environment across diverse categories.
competitions.fullDetails: Every project submitted must relate to the environment, with categories including science, engineering, robotics, business, art, short film, music, creative writing, and AI. Approximately 50% of submissions are accepted as finalists, and around 80% of finalists receive a medal. It attracts about 1,300 students from over 70 countries.
competitions.deadline: Check website for specific submission deadlines; National Tournament in May.
competitions.location: Hosted in New York (overall event); National Tournament in Los Angeles.
competitions.prizes: Medals, networking opportunities, and optional trips to D.C. or NYC.
competitions.fee: Application fee: $50; additional $425 if selected for in-person finals.
competitions.link: Check their website for regional dates.

competitions.title: 2026 National High School Design Competition (Cooper Hewitt)
competitions.shortDesc: A national design challenge for high school students to envision and design for their community's future, recognizing the U.S. 250th anniversary.
competitions.fullDetails: Organized by Cooper Hewitt, Smithsonian Design Museum, this competition invites high school students (grades 9-12 or homeschooled) to reflect on the nation's history and innovative spirit by designing for their community's future. Entries can be individual or from teams of up to three.
competitions.location: US-based (online submission); Virtual Regional Winners Day.
competitions.prizes: Five regional winners honored on the Cooper Hewitt website; select entries featured on an interactive map.

dailyInspiration.title: Teen Tech Founder Zach Yadegari Builds $30M AI Startup Before High School Graduation
dailyInspiration.story: Zach Yadegari, at just 18, was already a serial entrepreneur with a $30 million projected annual revenue AI startup, Cal AI. He began coding at age seven and had a six-figure exit from his first venture, 'Totally Science,' at 16. With a lean team, he launched Cal AI, an app that quickly garnered 5 million downloads by providing instant calorie and nutrition estimates from food photos, revolutionizing how users track their diet.
dailyInspiration.takeaway: Don't wait for perfect preparation; start before you're ready and focus on execution to solve real problems.
